introduction: A group of conditions with pathological processes resulting from the presence of Haemoglobin S. Usually inherited from the parents who have themselves inherited Haemoglobin S. The principal genotypes include: Homozygous sickle cell disease (SS), Sickle cell-haemoglobin C disease (SC), Sickle cell-B thalassaemia (SB thal), Sickle cell-B+ thalassaemia Type I (SB+thal. Type I), Sickle cell-B+ thalassaemia. Type II. (SB+thal Type II), Sickle cell-B+ thalassaemia. Type III. (SB+thal. Type III). Sickle cell trait is the inheritance of one normal gene controlling formation of B Haemoglobin (HbA), and a sickle gene (HbS). Total haemoglobin A is more than haemoglobin S. Normal haemoglobin F. Sickle cell disease is the inheritance of two abnormal allelemorphic genes controlling formation of B chains of haemoglobin, at least one of which is the sickle gene. Polymerization of the sickle haemoglobin may lead to vaso-occlusion.

features: Prominent facial bones due to increased bone marrow activity
Leaner body build and less weight (on average)
Acute symptoms/signs attributable to sickle cell disease
Cerebrovascular disease (in descending order of prevalence)
Hypoplastic (due to infection or renal failure)
Aplastic (e.g. due to epidemic parvo virus B19)

investigations: FBC and ESR; Red cell indices (MCH, MCHC, MCV), Reticulocyte count
Sickling tests: solubility test; metabisulphite test
Haemoglobin electrophoresis (using cellulose acetate paper at pH 8.4 or citrate agar gel at pH 5.6)
Liver function tests (transaminases, bilirubin, serum albumin, alkaline phosphatase and prothrombin time)
Sputum: Acid Fast Bacilli, Microscopy, culture and sensitivity
Stool: Ova and parasites, Occult blood

goals: Maintain (or restore) a steady state of health
Provide accurate diagnosis, relevant health education and genetic counselling to patients, relatives and heterozygotes
Provide a positive self-image in affected persons
non_drug: Adequate fluid intake (at least 3 litres/24 hours)
Avoidance of pain-inducing conditions (Strenous physical exertion or stress, Dehydration, Sudden exposure to extremes of temperature, Infections e.g. malaria, Emotional stress)
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Paracetamol should be used with caution in patients with hepatic impairment
Opioid analgesics cause respiratory depression and hypotension; should be avoided when intracranial pressure is suspected to be raised

differential_diagnoses: Connective tissue disorders e.g. rheumatoid arthritis
Other causes of failure to thrive
complications: Kidneys: Hyposthenuria, Haematuria, Albuminuria, Reduced kidney function
Leg ulcers: Occur around the ankles, Heal slowly and tend to recur
Bones and Joints: Osteomyelitis, Avascular necrosis
Infections: Salmonella osteomyelitis, Pneumococcal pneumonia, Pneumococcal meningitis (rare in adolescents and adults), Tonsillitis and pharyngitis
Brain and nerves: Strokes, seizures (not common in adults), Meningitis (not common in adults), Cerebral haemorrhage, Mental neuropathy (rare)
Cardiovascular/respiratory: Heart failure, Pulmonary hypertension, Acute chest syndrome
prevention: Advice on the risks involved in marriages between carriers and between sicklers
